speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 2. I'm interviewing Magda Klein. Mrs. Klein, when we left off you were telling me about men going into Jewish homes and coming out with bundles. Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually those were the gendarmes, the-- the policemens. So when we had seen that, being that my father served at the First World War, he was-- he was in First World War four years. As a matter of fact, when he came back, he didn't recognize his brother, because he got a new brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said that this is no good, so let's prepare. We didn't have knapsacks, so what we did, being that my older sister, Clara had a so-called-- I can't think of the name of it now. But before a girl gets married, they always prepare a lot of beddings and so on, so-- A dowry?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: A dowry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Something like that. So we had a lot of damask, down covers, and pillowcases. And in Europe, we used the big pillowcases. So what we did, my father said we should take the big pillowcase, put the potatoes in both corner as you're holding a pillowcase up, and take a string and tie it around, and leave enough material so that you are able to sew another pillow, a smaller pillowcase that you fold up and you sew it on on both end. Then on the top of the pillowcase, you fold it over and you made like a frame. And we put the string in it. Now, that end from the potatoes, where we sewed the material, we left it-- we swing it over, sew it on the other side. From this side, we sew it so it was a knapsack. And that's where we put in a lot of clothing and a lot of different things that we needed. So we made a knapsack for each one of us. And we worked on it very feverishly. Then we didn't know when is our turn that we will be leaving the house. So we had slept with two and three dresses on, figuring that this way, we don't know how much they let us take, so that we will have the dresses on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in April, from 15 until the 17th when they took us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. So we slept with our clothing on. And as I mentioned, my brother wasn't allowed to travel, so he came back and he had a knapsack, because we had bought a knapsack for him. My father had a knapsack, because he was called into the working battalion, so at that time he was outfitted with a raincoat, with a knapsack. Meantime, we had given some of the suits from my father and my brother and some of the better clothing and beddings and all this better things to our neighbor who wasn't Jewish, but her husband was a Jewish lawyer who was called into the working battalion and he was there and they had a little girl. So we knocked on the window and she took it in. I said-- I gave it to her and I said that we don't know what will be our future, but then we come back, then you will please give it back to us, because we will pay you for it, holding it. She said, oh, sure. Give me whatever you want. I will keep it for you. As a matter of fact, we even had a big basket full of eggs from-- from Passover that was left over. I hand it to her also. What happened is that finally it was our turn. They came to us. And when a-- two gendarme comes. With the two gendarme, the policemen, came some of the local so-called big shots to witness that-- what going on. And some of these three man who came with the gendarme, these were all friends of my father, that he served together in the First World War. In a way, they were so ashamed that they had to witness and they have to be there, so they were all sitting front of the house in one of this frame and talking and smoking and all that. The meantime, the-- the gendarme came in. And he said, Mr. Czigler-- Czigler [NON-ENGLISH], because that was the title in Europe, I want to see your family. So we lined up like an organ key. And he looked at us and we had our little earrings in our ear. Meantime, when we know that they are coming, my mother suggested that we should hide some of the jewelry, so my mother said, I'm going to give my diamond ring and my brother with the watch, because in Europe, that was the custom. When a boy got bar mitzvahed, they got a beautiful gold pocket watch with a gold chain, so my father had it. So when my mother said that, let's take the watch from Philip, and I will gave my rings and the bracelet and all the other thing-- so my father said, no, because they know that every Jewish woman have a diamond ring, and also every boy has a watch. But all the other jewelry that we had, like extra rings and things like that-- so my brother and myself we went up to the attic. And as you know, we had like these beams that holds the roof, so we put it in one of the joint. And we figured it out that there was like little window opening on the attic, so we figured it out that this will be the third-- third joint where we will look for it. And we told all my sisters and we told my parents that where is the jewelry. But the meantime, we didn't think about taking out the earrings from our ears, because it was so natural that-- so when the gendarmes had seen that we have the earrings and the-- so he said, I want you to give me all your jewelry, all your money, all the valuables, if you have any certificate, bonds, and this and that. And after the war, you will-- you will reimbursed for it or you will get it back, and they will give you a receipt for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took all the jewelry, everything, and we put it on the table. And one of my youngest-- my sister Eva, she was with my grandparents in another town for the second part of the holiday of-- of Passover. So we told him that we are-- we are missing one sister of mine. He made a note of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, he said, you know, Mr. Czigler, I like you very much. I like your family. He said, I would suggest that you take food with you, whatever you can, because you don't know how long you will be there and where you will be. So, he said, take you-- because the Jewish people was rendering the goose fat, and we had like different size of dishes that we were storing it. And, he said, if you have flour or if you have beans or whatever you have, take it with you. So naturally, we emptied it into some other pillowcases, the beans and the other things, flour, whatever we had. And the two little ones, they took a hold on the-- the fat dish and we marched out of our house. Then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They said that they want to re-settle us, because the war is going on and they need people to work in different places. That's what we were told. Then they took us to the shul and also to the-- to the elementary school too. So that was the first time that my eyes started to open up, because came the nighttime, you started to undress. You don't know if you should undress, you should not undress, and all these strange people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we were very shy, very family-oriented. I mean, we weren't like used to it, seeing naked men or naked-- things like that. So when I saw this man with his long [NON-ENGLISH], I said, oh my god. I mean, what-- what's going to happen? What's next.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the kids are crying. And one wants water. One wants milk and this and that. So that wasn't too bad, comparing. So we were there about three days. After that, when they collected everybody, then they were ordering all the farmers to come around with the horse and buggy. And we were seated in the-- in the bottom of the wagon, without the straw, without anything. So-- we needed one wagon ourself. So then we were seated there. And as we were going to Kisvarda, because that's where they took us, we passed our house. And we had seen the goose and the-- and the ducks and all that was going in. They were like making noise because they were hungry already. Nobody fed them, seems to me. So as we were passing, my mother said, I wonder if we will ever see our house again, where are they taking us, if we will ever return. This was exactly her word. When we arrived in Kisvarda, we had to get out of the wagon and go into the shul. Meantime, in the bottom, they had all the seats and everything, they were trying to empty it, put it on both sides. But that was full already, more or less, so we went upstairs, where the-- where the ladies section was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went up there and a lot of other people from Mandok was there also. As a matter of fact, it just turned out that our next neighbor on the floor was my teacher, my elementary school teacher, with his two daughter and his-- his son and the wife. And by that time, we started to get undressed. We just left one dress on ourself. So things were very mixed up and people didn't had food. And some people had, some people didn't had. So meantime, my brother went and he took out the potatoes from the knapsack and he started to make something. I don't know where he got the brick. But he put two-- two bricks on one side, two bricks on the other side. And he took one of the pot, put a little water in it, and he was cooking the potatoes what we had taken. That was the first meal that we had in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then as time progressed, more and more people were coming. The bottom part of the shul was like filled, people like sardines, one next to the other. One was crying. One was coughing. One was complaining. And meantime, my aunt, my mother younger sister, who lived in Kisvarda, she had heard what goes on and so on. And she knew that we were taken in, so she had workers-- because they were very well-to-do people, they had workers. And she had sent cook food and he gave it over the fence, so we had cook food. And then later on, when they were taken into the ghetto from their home, then she send message that we should go to the house where they are, because instead of to have strange people laying next to her-- because what they did, they closed off a certain area where it was mostly Jewish population, but there were also non-Jews who lived there. So they had relocated the non-Jews. And the meantime, the way they were gathering the people from all over the area, they were putting it into these houses. So my aunt was in one of the house like this. And we went over. We left our quarters in the shul and we went over to my aunt. And we made the room on the-- on the floor. And there was about four other families in the same room. But before you went into our room, you had to go through two, three other rooms, because this house belonged to a very rich family, so there were people that-- if you wanted to go to the bathroom, that you had to go through the bodies and you had to watch you shouldn't step over them. As a matter of fact, there was this family who were two boys and a girl and the mother. They had made my father-- being that he was the soldier, they made my father to be the responsible for the group of people in that building, because you had to make a name list that-- how many people and so on. So-- so we thought that maybe we will get some-- some ration ticket for-- for food and that's how they want to know it. So my father didn't want to leave anybody out, that in case they won't get their ticket. But as it turned out, they had gone into every Jewish house after we-- we were-- we left and they collected all the food. Like if you had jam, if you had other things, they collected it and they were bringing it into the ghetto. And they made a kitchen, a community kitchen. So they were volunteers who were cooking. And when they were bringing in the potatoes, they would put it down on the ground. Then they took one of the boy whom I had my eyes on him. He was the one who was put a whip in his hand that if anybody tried to steal the potatoes, that he should hit them.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I seen that, I get so disillusioned. And I said, my god, how could he even hold that whip in his hand? I mean, whom is he going to hit, his own kind, I mean. And he is-- and he was watching the potatoes. Can you give me an idea of how large this ghetto was, approximately how many blocks or buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say [LAUGHS] is-- is really very, very hard to say, because it was a very big territory, very big territory. I would say that from here to-- to Main Street. So is-- is very hard to figure it. They closed down the streets. Whenever they-- they acquired a house and they made it a Jewish ghetto, the streets were closed off. And there were policemen who were guarding it all along. And then they would ask the Jewish girls to go out and sweep the streets. So one day when I volunteered and I went out the sweep the street, I seen that the non-Jews are walking by and the life is going on. They go to the market and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came back, I said to my sister, Clara I said, you know, I think I could escape very easily and I could go back to Mandok and I could go to my mother friend, you know, Susan. And as I was whispering it to her, my mother overheard it. And she said, don't you dare to, don't you-- and I said, I didn't know you were listening. She said, I have ears all over. So I didn't-- I didn't do it. Then-- But it was-- was it a closed ghetto? You were not basically allowed to leave the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- you weren't allowed to leave the area. Every gate was closed. And the gendarme and the policemen, they were all walking around. They were patrolling the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Germans also, or were these all Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were stationed across the street. We-- we didn't had dealings with the Germans yet. It was strictly the good Hungarians, the good patriot Hungarians who know how to collect their Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any work assignments at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Was no work assignment. As the time was progressing, there were always more and more people who died, because there were sick people that they brought in and so on, so-- and there were volunteers who would go to the cemetery. And there was more volunteers who wanted to go to the cemetery, because what happened is the first time when they went, there is also a cemetery guard who was guarding the cemetery. And they made deals with the cemetery guard that he should have eggs and he should have milk and they would put it back into the casket, you know, because the Jewish people, they have like a carrier, a wooden carrier. And that's where they put the plaque with the-- and then they put the food in there, so there was like a-- a business extraction from the cemetery back to the ghetto. So a lot of people wanted to go to the cemetery to carry the dead, and this way they were able to bring back the food. And is really amazed me, I mean, that how resourceful people could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough food at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had enough food, because we getting the food from the kitchen and also the food that we have taken from home. And once you have fat and you have flour, then you could make anything. I mean, we-- we weren't hungry. We had-- as a matter of fact, my mother was giving food to other people who didn't bring in anything because they didn't have anything to bring in to begin with. Then, as time was progressing and spring was in the air-- because we were taken away from our home on the 17--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what--

speaker: interviewee
text: April, April 17, and this was already end of April and beginning of May. And the trees were starting to bud and spring is in the air. And as much as it was a lot of tsuris around, a lot of hardship, the lovers were lovers anywhere you looked. In the nighttime the bushes were rattling and they were walking hand-in-hand. I mean, it's really-- the-- the world doesn't stop. The life goes on. And there was the time when one of the gendarme came over, because we were next to their quarters, and they wanted somebody to clean up the house. So my father sent myself and my sister. So we went over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clara. And as we were there, we seen that the gendarme standing there with his hand in his hips. And we looked at each other. We just didn't like the way he looked at us. So I told him, I said, if you excuse me, sir, we are hungry. We would like to go back home and eat something. He said, OK, but not right away. He was thinking. When we got away, I said, oh, thank god. But we never talked about sex at home. I mean, it wasn't an open subject. I don't know why, but it wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened is that we told my brother that-- please go over and see what you could do there, because we don't feel comfortable there. We didn't tell him why, but he knew it. So then when we went home, my brother was the one who got up early in the morning when it was dark. He would go over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come nighttime he would come-- his shirt with the belt, you know, was packed up with bread. And what he did, before he would come home, he went over to the shul, where we were originally. And there were a lot of little kids, mothers who had no husband and no food. He would give them whatever he can, the bread. Also, he made a deal with one of the milk delivery woman who were bringing in milk for the gendarmes that he should bring more milk and he will buy it from her. And he used to hand it over the fence for the children, because they were hungry. They were-- they were thirsty. And he needed money. Now we used up our money. In the beginning, we gave the money for the milk. But then later on, we didn't have money. So we were asking around that if somebody has the money, give the money for the milk, because the-- the woman not going to deliver milk without money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of them gave money very reluctantly. Some of them didn't give the money. And we know that they have the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, my brother would even take milk over to the shul to the little kids. I mean, he was a marvelous, good-natured fellow, and very capable, very capable. He was the type that he find his way. Wherever it was, he find his way. And there was one day that one of the gendarme came over when we heard already that the other part of the ghetto was eliminated already. They had taken the people to the train station and they were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They said that we were-- we were resettled and we will be finding work and we will be working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened is that I had seen when I went to them-- because they had called people to gather the things what was left over in every room in every house. And I had seen that there was beautiful cakes left there. There were-- which meant that they were in a hurry, that they-- they were shipping them out. But when I say that they were in a hurry to shipping them out, well, my father used to tell us stories about his experience in-- during the war. And he would tell us that he was captured and he was stealing raw potatoes. So we children opened our mouth and we said that how could you eat raw potatoes? He said, my dear children, when you hungry, you are happy to have raw potatoes. Of course, we said, you didn't have a chance to cook it or bake it? He said no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he also said that whenever they were traveling, taken by the-- the-- the-- cattle train, the best thing is if you go in and you try to find the place that your back is against the side of the train, because if you are in the middle, people will step on you. And this way, you have support for you back. So when I had seen that, that these people were taken away, then it came to my mind when the Jewish people were taken away from Egypt that they were carrying the dough on their back, and that's why we celebrate Passover with the matzohs. I said, my goodness. History repeats itself. And I told my mother, I said, they taken away many of the valuables. Good food was left over, which means it must have been in a hurry. So I said, they going to take us soon also. So we were getting ready. We-- we were preparing ourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because by that time, you got so hardened that it was a natural thing, that because you are a Jew-- because you are a Jew. No crime was committed, but you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, the gendarme comes over. And he is looking for Czigler [NON-ENGLISH], which means Mrs. Czigler. And my mother said, yes. He said, are you Philip's mother? So my mother said yes. And she couldn't imagine what does the gendarme want.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look, I like him. I like Philip very much, like my own brother. And we have a very big land. We are farmers. And my mother is all alone and Philip could help her a lot. And I would like to give him one of my uniform and he will give-- he would go as me. He would travel like if it was me. And he would stay with my mother. I would write a letter to her and she would understand. And she would be very happy and very grateful to you, Mrs. Czigler, if Philip could go there. My mother looked at him and she said, you know, we don't know what's our futures, what will be the future. Far as we know, they want us to work. And he is the most capable among us. My husband is over 40 and he is a young man, so we will need him, because we don't know what it will be.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm sorry, but I have to say no. Wherever we go, he will come with us. So he said, well, I'm very sorry, Mrs. Czigler, that you decided this way, but it's your decision. And I'm going to come back and I'm going to bring you something. So he came back. He bought a big, big can with a handle, you know, like a water pitcher, full of delicious black, but black coffee, sweet like honey. He said, you will be traveling I don't know for how many days, and you might get water. You might not get water. So this way, you will have what to drink and will quenches your thirst.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, thank you very much. I really appreciate that. So after he left, my mother said, who knows how many days we're going to be walking or traveling if he said that. So sure enough, next day they made the announcement that everybody should pack and go to the lineup. And they were counting you off and all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? How long had you been in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the ghetto about-- almost six weeks, almost six weeks. Meantime, just the night before we were taken away, this young man who looked like a Gypsy-- because, you know, some of the Jews, they were dark-skinned, dark, curly hair-- he came to my father and he said, Czigler [NON-ENGLISH], I'm going to disappear. You never knew me. You never seen me. You don't know where I am, if I died, or if I went with my other-- with the other transfer. I'm not available, but I want you to know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother-- my father said, OK. So he dressed up as a Gypsy and he even blackened his face more. And during the night, the night before we were taken away, he just disappeared. I never seen him. I have to break for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
